meta.name: Omega
meta.title: Omega CN1500 Multi-Zone Controller
meta.description: Live OCR of an Omega CN1500 ramp & soak controller. The single 7-segment display auto-scans through up to 7 channels; each value is followed by a per-channel unit-suffix that uniquely identifies which channel it belongs to.

llm.prompt:
These photos are of the front panel of an Omega CN1500 multi-zone temperature controller. The single 7-segment LED display auto-scans through the enabled channels, showing each one for a few seconds before advancing.

Right now we only have channel 1 and channel 6 connected. Make sure to only display those two channels!

In each scan slot the display first shows a label of the form "Ctr# Pr" (where # is 1..7 and "Pr" stands for "process value"), then the numeric temperature followed by a 2-character engineering-units suffix in the format "C X". The first character is always 'C' (Centigrade). The second character is a UNIT-SUFFIX TAG that uniquely identifies which channel the value belongs to:

  C A → ctr1   (temperature for channel 1, unit-tag 'A')
  C b → ctr2   (channel 2, unit-tag 'b')
  C C → ctr3   (channel 3, unit-tag 'C' — note that the first character is also 'C' for Centigrade; the SECOND character is the channel tag)
  C d → ctr4   (channel 4, unit-tag 'd')
  C E → ctr5   (channel 5, unit-tag 'E')
  C F → ctr6   (channel 6, unit-tag 'F')
  C G → ctr7   (channel 7, unit-tag 'G')

USE THE SECOND CHARACTER OF THE SUFFIX as the primary channel identifier — it is shown at the same time as the value, so it's unambiguous. The "Ctr# Pr" label that flashes just before the value is a secondary confirmation only; if it's not visible in any frame, rely on the unit-tag.

Across all the frames provided, identify every (channel, value) pair you can read clearly and return a JSON object mapping channel name to the numeric Celsius value. Use null for any channel that does not appear, or whose value you cannot read confidently, in any of the frames.
llm.example_prompt:
These photos are of the front panel of an Omega CN1500 multi-zone temperature controller. The single 7-segment LED display auto-scans through the enabled channels, showing each one for a few seconds before advancing.

In each scan slot the display first shows a label of the form "Ctr# Pr" (where # is 1..7 and "Pr" stands for "process value"), then the numeric temperature followed by a 2-character engineering-units suffix in the format "C X". The first character is always 'C' (Centigrade). The second character is a UNIT-SUFFIX TAG that uniquely identifies which channel the value belongs to:

  C A → ctr1   (temperature for channel 1, unit-tag 'A')
  C b → ctr2   (channel 2, unit-tag 'b')
  C C → ctr3   (channel 3, unit-tag 'C' — note that the first character is also 'C' for Centigrade; the SECOND character is the channel tag)
  C d → ctr4   (channel 4, unit-tag 'd')
  C E → ctr5   (channel 5, unit-tag 'E')
  C F → ctr6   (channel 6, unit-tag 'F')
  C G → ctr7   (channel 7, unit-tag 'G')

USE THE SECOND CHARACTER OF THE SUFFIX as the primary channel identifier — it is shown at the same time as the value, so it's unambiguous. The "Ctr# Pr" label that flashes just before the value is a secondary confirmation only; if it's not visible in any frame, rely on the unit-tag.

Across all the frames provided, identify every (channel, value) pair you can read clearly and return a JSON object mapping channel name to the numeric Celsius value. Use null for any channel that does not appear, or whose value you cannot read confidently, in any of the frames.
